text.title: Docker Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules for the docker methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale + concrete remediation hook.

rule.statement: Production Dockerfiles for compiled languages MUST use multi-stage builds and copy only the final binary/artefacts into a distroless or minimal base.
rule.rationale: Single-stage images include build toolchains, source, and dev dependencies that bloat the image and widen the attack surface.

rule.statement: Production images MUST set a non-root `USER` and include a `HEALTHCHECK` instruction.
rule.rationale: Running as root inside a container amplifies escape risk; missing healthchecks blind orchestrators to crash-loops.

rule.statement: Base images MUST be pinned to an immutable digest (`@sha256:...`); floating tags like `:latest` are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Floating tags break reproducibility and let upstream image swaps inject untracked changes.

rule.statement: Every Dockerfile MUST be accompanied by a `.dockerignore` covering `.git`, secrets, build artefacts, and node_modules / venv equivalents.
rule.rationale: Without `.dockerignore` secrets and large directories leak into the build context, balloon image size, and risk credential exfiltration.

rule.statement: CI builds for dependency-heavy stacks (pip, npm, apt) MUST use BuildKit cache mounts (`--mount=type=cache`).
rule.rationale: Without cache mounts, every CI build re-downloads dependencies, multiplying build minutes and bandwidth cost.

rule.statement: Images MUST be scanned by Artifact Analysis (or Trivy/Grype) before push to production registries; CRITICAL CVEs MUST block the push.
rule.rationale: Pushing unscanned images bypasses the only automated gate that catches known-vulnerable base images.

rule.statement: If the input fails ALL "Applies If" preconditions or matches ANY "Skip If" disqualifier, do not apply this methodology — route the task to the adjacent sibling instead.
rule.rationale: Even flat checklists need an explicit skip leaf in the decision tree; this rule is the universal terminal.
